---
title: "ARC Algorithm Pseudocode Reference"
subtitle: "Adaptive Regularization using Cubics"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    header-includes:
      - \usepackage{algorithm}
      - \usepackage{algpseudocode}
      - \usepackage{amsmath}
      - \usepackage{amssymb}
---

# Introduction

This document provides implementation-ready pseudocode for the arcopt package, which implements Adaptive Regularization using Cubics (ARC). A programmer unfamiliar with the project but knowledgeable in optimization should be able to implement the complete system from these specifications.

## Architecture Overview

The optimizer consists of **9 algorithm groups** organized into 4 layers:

| Layer | Components | Purpose |
|-------|------------|---------|
| **Orchestration** | Algorithm 1 (Main Loop) | Controls iteration flow, dispatches to sub-algorithms |
| **Core Solvers** | Algorithms 1a, 5 | Newton step, cubic subproblem solver |
| **Adaptation** | Algorithms 0, 2a/2b, 3, 4 | Convergence, σ updates, momentum, quasi-Newton |
| **Safeguards** | Algorithms 6a/6b, 7, 8 | Robustness, constraints |

## System Flowchart

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 8
%%| fig-height: 10
flowchart TD
    subgraph INIT["Initialization"]
        A[x₀, σ₀, parameters] --> A2{fnscale ≠ 1?}
        A2 -->|Yes| A3[Wrap f, g, H<br/>with fnscale]
        A2 -->|No| B
        A3 --> B
        B{Constraints?}
        B -->|Box| C[Validate & project x₀<br/>Algorithm 7b]
        B -->|Linear Eq| D[Compute null space Z<br/>Transform to z-space<br/>Algorithm 8a-b]
        B -->|None| E[Use x₀ directly]
        C --> F[Main Loop]
        D --> F
        E --> F
    end

    subgraph MAIN["Main Loop (Algorithm 1)"]
        F --> G{Previous step<br/>rejected?}
        G -->|Yes| H[Skip evaluation<br/>Reuse f, g, H]
        G -->|No| I[Evaluate f, g, H]

        I --> J[Convergence Check<br/>Algorithm 0]
        H --> K[Cubic Subproblem]
        J -->|Converged| DONE[Return x*]
        J -->|Continue| L{Quasi-Newton?}

        L -->|Yes| K
        L -->|No| M[Try Newton Step<br/>Algorithm 1a]

        M -->|Success| N[Accept Newton step<br/>Decrease σ]
        M -->|Fail| K
    end

    subgraph CUBIC["Cubic Subproblem (Algorithm 5)"]
        K --> P[Modified Cholesky Solver<br/>LDL → iterate λ]
        P --> R[Step s]
    end

    subgraph EVAL["Step Evaluation"]
        R --> S{Box<br/>constraints?}
        S -->|Yes| T[Truncate step<br/>Algorithm 7a]
        S -->|No| U[Use s directly]
        T --> V[Evaluate f_trial]
        U --> V
        V --> W[Compute ρ = actual/predicted]
    end

    subgraph UPDATE["Updates"]
        W --> X{ρ ≥ η₁?}
        X -->|Yes| Y[Accept: x ← x + s]
        X -->|No| Z[Reject: x unchanged<br/>Mark rejected]

        Y --> AA[Update σ<br/>Algorithm 2a/2b]
        Z --> AA

        AA --> AB{Using<br/>momentum?}
        AB -->|Yes| AC[Apply momentum<br/>Algorithm 3]
        AB -->|No| AD[Continue]
        AC --> AD

        AD --> AE{Using SR1?}
        AE -->|Yes| AF[Update B<br/>Algorithm 4]
        AE -->|No| AG[Continue]
        AF --> AG

        AG --> AH{NaN/Inf<br/>detected?}
        AH -->|Yes| AI[Recovery<br/>Algorithm 6b]
        AH -->|No| AJ{Stagnation?}
        AI --> F
        AJ -->|Yes| AK[Recovery<br/>Algorithm 6a]
        AJ -->|No| F
        AK --> F
    end

    N --> AA

    style INIT fill:#e1f5fe
    style MAIN fill:#fff3e0
    style CUBIC fill:#f3e5f5
    style EVAL fill:#e8f5e9
    style UPDATE fill:#fce4ec
```

## Algorithm Index

| Algorithm | Name | Section | Purpose |
|-----------|------|---------|---------|
| 0 | Convergence Check | §3 | Stan-style multi-criteria termination |
| 1 | Main ARC Loop | §4 | Orchestrates Newton-first + cubic fallback |
| 1a | Newton Step | §4.2 | Gershgorin pre-check + Cholesky solve |
| 2a | CGT Update | §5.1 | Classical σ adaptation |
| 2b | Interpolation Update | §5.2 | Lipschitz-enhanced σ adaptation |
| 3 | ARCm | §6 | Momentum acceleration |
| 4 | SR1 Update | §7 | Quasi-Newton with safeguards |
| 5 | Modified Cholesky Solver | §8 | Cubic subproblem via LDL + λ iteration |
| 6a | Stagnation Recovery | §9.1 | Detect/recover from stagnation |
| 6b | NaN/Inf Protection | §9.2 | Handle numerical failures |
| 7a | Box Step Truncation | §10.1 | Apply bounds to step |
| 7b | Initial Point Projection | §10.1 | Validate starting point |
| 8a | Null Space Basis | §10.2 | Compute Z for linear equality |
| 8b | Space Transforms | §10.2 | Convert between x and z spaces |
| 8c | Constraint Projection | §10.2 | Project to feasible manifold |

## Computational Complexity

The table below shows per-call complexity. For the main loop (Algorithm 1), multiply by number of iterations K.

| Algorithm | Operations | f calls | g calls | H calls | Frequency | C++ Benefit |
|-----------|------------|---------|---------|---------|-----------|-------------|
| 0 | O(n) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1/iter | Low (vectorized) |
| 1 | *orchestration* | 1-2 | 1 | 0-1 | — | Low |
| 1a | **O(n³)** | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0-1/iter | **Medium** (Gershgorin loop) |
| 2a | O(1) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1/iter | None |
| 2b | O(1) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1/iter | None |
| 3 | O(n) | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1/iter | Low |
| 4 | O(n²) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1/iter | Low (BLAS) |
| 5 | **O(n³)×L** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0-1/iter | Low (LAPACK ldl/chol) |
| 6a | O(1) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1/iter | None |
| 6b | O(n³)×R | R | R | 0 | rare | Low |
| 7a | O(n) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1/iter | **Medium** (tight loop) |
| 7b | O(n) | 0 | 0 | 0 | once | Low |
| 8a | O(n³) | 0 | 0 | 0 | once | Low (LAPACK QR) |
| 8b | O(n·d) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 2/iter | Low (BLAS) |
| 8c | O(m³) | 0 | 0 | 0 | rare | Low (LAPACK) |

**Legend:**

- **L** = λ iterations in Cholesky solver (typically 3-10)
- **R** = NaN retries (typically 0-3)
- **d** = n - rank(A), reduced dimension for linear equality constraints
- **m** = number of linear equality constraints
- **f/g/H calls** = calls to user-provided objective, gradient, Hessian functions

**C++ Benefit Assessment:**

| Rating | Meaning | Examples |
|--------|---------|----------|
| **None** | O(1) operations, no loops | 2a, 2b, 6a |
| **Low** | Already uses BLAS/LAPACK or simple vectorized ops | 0, 3, 4, 5, 8a-c |
| **Medium** | Tight loops over n elements, not BLAS-able | 1a (Gershgorin), 7a (bounds) |
| **High** | Would require many R iterations | *None in current design* |

**Key Insight**: The expensive O(n³) operations (LDL/Cholesky in 5, QR in 8a) already call LAPACK through R's `fastmatrix::ldl()`, `chol()`, and `qr()` functions. C++ would not significantly improve these.

**Where C++ helps most:**

1. **Gershgorin bound (Algorithm 1a)**: O(n²/2) loop over matrix elements
2. **Box constraint truncation (Algorithm 7a)**: O(n) loop with conditionals

These are candidates for Rcpp if profiling shows them as bottlenecks.

## Notation

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $x_k \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Current iterate |
| $f_k = f(x_k)$ | Function value |
| $g_k = \nabla f(x_k)$ | Gradient |
| $H_k = \nabla^2 f(x_k)$ | Hessian (or approximation $B_k$) |
| $\sigma_k > 0$ | Regularization parameter |
| $s_k$ | Step (search direction) |
| $\rho_k$ | Ratio of actual to predicted reduction |
| $\lambda$ | Cubic subproblem multiplier ($\lambda = \sigma\|s\|$) |
| $m_k(s)$ | Cubic model: $f_k + g_k^T s + \frac{1}{2} s^T H_k s + \frac{\sigma_k}{3}\|s\|^3$ |

## Execution Flow Summary

1. **Initialize**: Set $x_0$, $\sigma_0$; handle constraints if present
2. **Evaluate**: Compute $f_k$, $g_k$, $H_k$ (skip if previous step rejected)
3. **Check convergence**: 6 criteria (gradient, objective change, parameter change)
4. **Compute step**:
   - If analytic/FD Hessian: try Newton first (Gershgorin → Cholesky → Armijo)
   - If Newton fails or quasi-Newton: solve cubic subproblem
5. **Evaluate step**: Compute $\rho_k$; accept if $\rho_k \geq \eta_1$
6. **Update**: Adjust $\sigma_k$; apply momentum/SR1 if enabled; check safeguards
7. **Repeat** until converged

## Theoretical Guarantees

Unlike line search methods (which use Wolfe conditions), ARC convergence relies on the **ratio test** and **regularization bounds**. The following conditions must hold for the theoretical complexity guarantees of Cartis, Gould, and Toint (2011).

### Required Assumptions

| Assumption | Mathematical Statement | Practical Check |
|------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| **A1**: Bounded below | $f(x) \geq f_{\text{low}}$ for all $x$ | Monitor $f_k$; warn if approaching machine limits |
| **A2**: Smooth objective | $f \in C^2$ (twice continuously differentiable) | User responsibility; check for NaN/Inf in $g$, $H$ |
| **A3**: Lipschitz Hessian | $\|\nabla^2 f(x) - \nabla^2 f(y)\| \leq L_H \|x - y\|$ | Cannot verify directly; assume holds |
| **A4**: Bounded $\sigma$ | $0 < \sigma_{\min} \leq \sigma_k \leq \sigma_{\max} < \infty$ | Enforced by Algorithm 2a/2b |
| **A5**: Subproblem accuracy | $s_k$ is an approximate global minimizer of $m_k(s)$ | Ensured by Algorithms 5a/5b |

### Guaranteed Complexity (Cartis-Gould-Toint 2011)

Under assumptions A1-A5:

| Target | Iterations to Achieve | Meaning |
|--------|----------------------|---------|
| $\|\nabla f(x_k)\| \leq \epsilon$ | $O(\epsilon^{-3/2})$ | First-order criticality |
| $\|\nabla f(x_k)\| \leq \epsilon$ and $\nabla^2 f(x_k) \succeq -\epsilon I$ | $O(\epsilon^{-3})$ | Second-order criticality |

This improves on trust-region/line-search methods which require $O(\epsilon^{-2})$ for first-order.

### Runtime Monitoring

The optimizer tracks these conditions at runtime and issues warnings when violations are detected. See **Algorithm 1, Step 9** for implementation details.

**Monitored conditions:**

- **Boundedness**: Warn if $f_k$ approaches machine limits
- **Smoothness**: Warn if NaN/Inf detected in derivatives
- **Ratio health**: Warn on repeated $\rho_k < 0$ (indicates model quality degradation)
- **Sigma saturation**: Warn if $\sigma_k = \sigma_{\max}$ but steps still rejected
- **Step quality**: Error if predicted reduction $\leq 0$ (subproblem solver failure)

### Comparison with Wolfe Conditions

| Aspect | Line Search (Wolfe) | ARC (Ratio Test) |
|--------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Sufficient decrease** | $f(x_k + \alpha s) \leq f_k + c_1 \alpha g_k^T s$ | $\rho_k = \frac{f_k - f_{k+1}}{m_k(0) - m_k(s_k)} \geq \eta_1$ |
| **Curvature condition** | $g(x_k + \alpha s)^T s \geq c_2 g_k^T s$ | *Not needed*: cubic term handles curvature |
| **Step length** | Found by line search ($\alpha$) | Implicit in $\sigma$: larger $\sigma$ → shorter step |
| **Guarantees** | Global convergence | Global convergence + complexity bounds |
| **Parameters** | $c_1 \in (0, 1)$, $c_2 \in (c_1, 1)$ | $\eta_1 \in (0, 1)$, $\eta_2 \in (\eta_1, 1)$ |

**Key difference**: Wolfe conditions constrain the *step length* $\alpha$; ARC constrains the *model fidelity* via $\rho_k$. The regularization parameter $\sigma_k$ implicitly controls step size through the cubic term $\frac{\sigma}{3}\|s\|^3$.

# Diagnostics and Output

The optimizer provides two mechanisms for monitoring and debugging: **refresh** controls console output during optimization, and **trace** controls what diagnostic information is stored for post-hoc analysis.

## Refresh (Console Output)

Following Stan's convention, `refresh` controls how often iteration summaries are printed:

| `refresh` | Behavior |
|-----------|----------|
| 0 | Silent (no output) |
| 1 | Print every iteration |
| N | Print every N iterations |

**Output format:**

```
iter      f(x)        ||g||_inf      σ          step_type    time
   1   1.234e+02      5.67e-01   1.00e+00       newton      0.01s
   2   8.901e+01      3.21e-02   5.00e-01       cubic       0.02s
  10   2.345e-03      1.23e-06   1.25e-01       newton      0.01s
```

## Trace (Diagnostic Storage)

The `trace` parameter controls what information is stored per iteration for post-hoc analysis:

| Level | Stored | Memory |
|-------|--------|--------|
| 0 | Nothing (only final result) | O(n) |
| 1 | `f_k`, `||g_k||`, `converge_reason` | O(K) |
| 2 | + `σ_k`, `ρ_k`, `step_type`, `solver_used`, `iter_time` | O(K) |
| 3 | + `x_k`, `s_k`, `H_k`, all convergence criteria, SR1 diagnostics, `β_k` | O(Kn²) |

where K = number of iterations.

**Return structure** (when `trace ≥ 1`):

```r
result$trace <- list(
  # Level 1
  f = numeric(K),           # Function values
  g_norm = numeric(K),      # Gradient norms (infinity)
  converge_reason = character(K),  # Which criterion checked


  # Level 2 (additional)
  sigma = numeric(K),       # Regularization parameter
  rho = numeric(K),         # Actual/predicted reduction ratio
  step_type = character(K), # "newton", "cubic"
  lambda = numeric(K),      # Cubic subproblem multiplier (λ = σ||s||)
  time = numeric(K),        # Wall-clock seconds per iteration

  # Level 3 (additional)
  x = matrix(K, n),         # Iterates (K × n)
  s = matrix(K, n),         # Steps (K × n)
  H = array(K, n, n),       # Hessians (K × n × n)
  converge_criteria = list(...),  # All 6 convergence values per iteration
  sr1_skip_count = integer(K),    # SR1 skipped updates (if using SR1)
  beta = numeric(K)         # Momentum parameter (if using momentum)
)
```

**Default Parameters:**

| Parameter | Default | Description |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| `refresh` | 10 | Print every 10 iterations (0 = silent) |
| `trace` | 1 | Store basic diagnostics |

# Convergence Checking

The optimizer uses multiple convergence criteria following Stan's L-BFGS implementation. Termination occurs when **any** criterion is satisfied. This multi-criteria approach prevents premature termination on ill-scaled problems while ensuring robust detection of true convergence.

*Cross-reference: See literature-review.qmd Section 5 (Safeguarding) for discussion of convergence tolerances and stagnation detection.*

## Algorithm 0: Convergence Check (Stan-style)

```
ALGORITHM 0: Convergence Check
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  g_k             Current gradient
  f_k, f_{k-1}    Current and previous function values
  x_k, x_{k-1}    Current and previous iterates
  tol_grad        Absolute gradient tolerance
  tol_rel_grad    Relative gradient tolerance
  tol_obj         Absolute objective change tolerance
  tol_rel_obj     Relative objective change tolerance
  tol_param       Parameter change tolerance
  k               Iteration count
  max_iter        Maximum iterations

OUTPUT:
  converged       TRUE if any criterion satisfied
  reason          String describing which criterion triggered

PROCEDURE:
  1. CHECK ITERATION LIMIT:
     IF k ≥ max_iter THEN
       RETURN (TRUE, "max_iter")
     END IF

  2. CHECK GRADIENT NORM (absolute):
     IF ||g_k||_∞ < tol_grad THEN
       RETURN (TRUE, "gradient_abs")
     END IF

  3. CHECK GRADIENT NORM (relative):
     IF ||g_k||_∞ < tol_rel_grad × max(1, |f_k|) THEN
       RETURN (TRUE, "gradient_rel")
     END IF

  4. CHECK OBJECTIVE CHANGE (absolute):
     IF k > 0 AND |f_k - f_{k-1}| < tol_obj THEN
       RETURN (TRUE, "objective_abs")
     END IF

  5. CHECK OBJECTIVE CHANGE (relative):
     IF k > 0 AND |f_k - f_{k-1}| < tol_rel_obj × max(1, |f_k|) THEN
       RETURN (TRUE, "objective_rel")
     END IF

  6. CHECK PARAMETER CHANGE:
     IF k > 0 AND ||x_k - x_{k-1}||_∞ < tol_param THEN
       RETURN (TRUE, "parameter")
     END IF

  RETURN (FALSE, "")
```

**Default Parameters** (following Stan):

| Parameter | Default | Description |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| `tol_grad` | $10^{-8}$ | Absolute gradient tolerance |
| `tol_rel_grad` | $10^{7}$ | Relative gradient tolerance |
| `tol_obj` | $10^{-12}$ | Absolute objective change tolerance |
| `tol_rel_obj` | $10^{4}$ | Relative objective change tolerance |
| `tol_param` | $10^{-8}$ | Parameter change tolerance |
| `max_iter` | 1000 | Maximum iterations |

# Core ARC Algorithm

The main optimization loop attempts a Newton step first when conditions are favorable, falling back to cubic regularization when needed. This hybrid approach combines Newton's fast local convergence with ARC's robustness to indefinite Hessians.

*Cross-reference: See literature-review.qmd Section 1 (Introduction) for motivation of the cubic model, and Section 2 for regularization parameter adaptation strategies.*

## Algorithm 1: ARC with Newton-First Strategy

```
ALGORITHM 1: ARC with Newton-First Strategy
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  x_0           Initial point
  σ_0           Initial regularization parameter
  hess_method   Hessian source: "analytic", "fd", "sr1", or "bfgs"
  η_1, η_2      Acceptance thresholds
  γ             Multiplicative update factor
  σ_min, σ_max  Regularization bounds
  c_armijo      Armijo sufficient decrease parameter (e.g., 1e-4)
  fnscale       Objective scaling (1 = minimize, -1 = maximize)
  tol_lambda    Cubic solver λ tolerance (e.g., 1e-10)
  max_iter_lambda  Cubic solver max iterations (e.g., 100)
  refresh       Print frequency (0 = silent, N = every N iterations)
  trace         Diagnostic level (0-3)

OUTPUT:
  x*            Approximate minimizer (or maximizer if fnscale = -1)
  f*            Function value at x* (original scale)
  g*            Gradient at x* (original scale)
  H*            Hessian at x* (original scale, or B* if quasi-Newton)
  converge_info Convergence status and reason
  trace_data    Diagnostic history (if trace ≥ 1)

PROCEDURE:
  [WRAPPER: Apply fnscale for maximization support]
  IF fnscale ≠ 1 THEN
    f_orig ← f
    g_orig ← g
    H_orig ← H
    f ← λx. fnscale × f_orig(x)
    g ← λx. fnscale × g_orig(x)
    H ← λx. fnscale × H_orig(x)
  END IF

  k ← 0
  is_qn ← (hess_method ∈ {"sr1", "bfgs"})
  prev_rejected ← FALSE              [track if previous step was rejected]
  consecutive_negative_rho ← 0       [for monitoring theoretical guarantees]
  IF trace ≥ 1 THEN initialize_trace_storage() END IF

  WHILE NOT converged DO
    iter_start_time ← clock()

    1. EVALUATE (skip if previous step was rejected):
       IF NOT prev_rejected THEN
         f_k ← f(x_k)
         g_k ← ∇f(x_k)
         H_k ← get_hessian(x_k, hess_method)    [or B_k if quasi-Newton]
       END IF
       [If prev_rejected: f_k, g_k, H_k unchanged from previous iteration]

    2. CHECK CONVERGENCE:
       (converged, reason) ← convergence_check(...)    [Algorithm 0]
       IF converged THEN BREAK END IF

    3. ATTEMPT NEWTON STEP (skip if rejected or quasi-Newton):
       step_type ← "cubic"    [default]
       IF NOT prev_rejected AND NOT is_qn THEN
         newton_success ← try_newton_step(g_k, H_k, x_k, f_k, c_armijo)
         IF newton_success THEN
           x_{k+1} ← x_k + s_newton
           s_k ← s_newton
           σ_{k+1} ← max(σ_k / γ, σ_min)    [successful: decrease σ]
           ρ_k ← NA    [not computed for Newton]
           step_type ← "newton"
           prev_rejected ← FALSE
           GOTO STEP 9    [skip cubic machinery]
         END IF
       END IF
       [If prev_rejected: Newton already failed with this H_k, skip]

    4. CUBIC SUBPROBLEM (Newton failed, rejected, or quasi-Newton):
       (s_k, λ_k) ← solve_cubic_modified_cholesky(g_k, H_k, σ_k, tol_lambda, max_iter_lambda)
       [Algorithm 5; handles both PD and indefinite H via mchol]

    5. EVALUATE TRIAL POINT:
       x_trial ← x_k + s_k
       f_trial ← f(x_trial)

    6. COMPUTE RATIO:
       pred_reduction ← m_k(0) - m_k(s_k)
       IF pred_reduction ≤ 0 THEN
         ERROR("Cubic subproblem solver failed: predicted reduction non-positive")
       END IF
       actual_reduction ← f_k - f_trial
       ρ_k ← actual_reduction / pred_reduction

    7. UPDATE ITERATE AND TRACK REJECTION:
       IF ρ_k ≥ η_1 THEN
         x_{k+1} ← x_trial    [accept step]
         prev_rejected ← FALSE
       ELSE
         x_{k+1} ← x_k        [reject step]
         prev_rejected ← TRUE
         [Next iteration: skip eval, skip Newton, re-solve cubic with larger σ]
       END IF

    8. UPDATE REGULARIZATION:
       σ_{k+1} ← update_sigma(σ_k, ρ_k, η_1, η_2, γ, σ_min, σ_max)
       [Algorithm 2a or 2b]

    9. DIAGNOSTICS AND MONITORING:
       iter_time ← clock() - iter_start_time

       [MONITORING: Check theoretical guarantee conditions]
       IF f_k < f_low_warn THEN
         WARN("Objective approaching lower bound limit")
       END IF

       IF ρ_k < 0 THEN
         consecutive_negative_rho ← consecutive_negative_rho + 1
         IF consecutive_negative_rho > 3 THEN
           WARN("Repeated ρ < 0: model quality degraded, L_H assumption may be violated")
         END IF
       ELSE
         consecutive_negative_rho ← 0
       END IF

       IF σ_k = σ_max AND ρ_k < η_1 THEN
         WARN("σ saturated at maximum but steps still rejected")
       END IF

       [TRACE: Store iteration data]
       IF trace ≥ 1 THEN
         store_trace_level1(k, f_k, ||g_k||_∞, reason)
       END IF
       IF trace ≥ 2 THEN
         store_trace_level2(k, σ_k, ρ_k, step_type, iter_time)
       END IF
       IF trace ≥ 3 THEN
         store_trace_level3(k, x_k, s_k, H_k, converge_criteria, ...)
       END IF

       [REFRESH: Print progress]
       IF refresh > 0 AND k MOD refresh = 0 THEN
         PRINT iter=k, f=f_k, ||g||=||g_k||_∞, σ=σ_k, step=step_type, time=iter_time
       END IF

    k ← k + 1
  END WHILE

  [Return original-scale values]
  IF fnscale ≠ 1 THEN
    f_k ← f_k / fnscale    [convert back to original scale]
    g_k ← g_k / fnscale
    H_k ← H_k / fnscale
  END IF

  RETURN x_k, f_k, g_k, H_k, converge_info
```

## Algorithm 1a: Newton Step with Gershgorin Pre-Check

This subroutine attempts a Newton step, using Gershgorin's theorem as a cheap pre-check for positive definiteness before the more expensive Cholesky factorization.

*Cross-reference: See literature-review.qmd Section 5.1 for Gershgorin pre-check discussion.*

```
ALGORITHM 1a: Try Newton Step
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  g_k         Gradient
  H_k         Hessian (analytic or FD, NOT quasi-Newton)
  x_k         Current iterate
  f_k         Current function value
  c_armijo    Armijo parameter (e.g., 1e-4)

OUTPUT:
  success     TRUE if Newton step accepted
  s_newton    Newton step (if successful)

PROCEDURE:
  1. GERSHGORIN PRE-CHECK:
     λ_min_bound ← gershgorin_lower_bound(H_k)    [O(n²)]
     IF λ_min_bound ≤ 0 THEN
       RETURN (FALSE, NULL)    [H_k may be indefinite, skip Newton]
     END IF

  2. ATTEMPT CHOLESKY:
     (L, chol_success) ← cholesky(H_k)
     IF NOT chol_success THEN
       RETURN (FALSE, NULL)    [H_k is indefinite]
     END IF

  3. SOLVE NEWTON SYSTEM:
     s_newton ← -L^{-T} L^{-1} g_k    [i.e., s = -H_k^{-1} g_k]

  4. CHECK ARMIJO CONDITION:
     f_trial ← f(x_k + s_newton)
     IF f_trial ≤ f_k + c_armijo × g_k^T s_newton THEN
       RETURN (TRUE, s_newton)    [Newton step accepted]
     END IF

  RETURN (FALSE, NULL)    [Newton step rejected]


FUNCTION gershgorin_lower_bound(H):
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  [Cheap O(n²/2) lower bound on minimum eigenvalue for symmetric H]

  n ← dim(H)
  row_sums ← zeros(n)

  [Exploit symmetry: traverse upper triangle only]
  FOR i = 1 TO n DO
    FOR j = i+1 TO n DO
      val ← |H_{ij}|
      row_sums[i] ← row_sums[i] + val
      row_sums[j] ← row_sums[j] + val    [H_{ji} = H_{ij}]
    END FOR
  END FOR

  [Compute minimum Gershgorin lower bound]
  min_lower ← +∞
  FOR i = 1 TO n DO
    lower_i ← H_{ii} - row_sums[i]
    min_lower ← min(min_lower, lower_i)
  END FOR

  RETURN min_lower

  [If min_lower > 0, H is guaranteed positive definite (sufficient but not necessary)]
```

**Why Newton-First?**

- Newton converges quadratically near solutions; ARC adds overhead when Newton works
- Gershgorin check is O(n²) vs Cholesky's O(n³) — cheap to try first
- Only attempt Newton with analytic/FD Hessians; SR1 approximations may be intentionally indefinite

# Regularization Parameter Updates

The regularization parameter $\sigma_k$ controls the trade-off between the quadratic model and the cubic safeguard. These algorithms adapt $\sigma_k$ based on how well the model predicted actual function decrease.

*Cross-reference: See literature-review.qmd Section 2 for detailed comparison of CGT (Option 1) and Interpolation-Enhanced (Option 2) adaptation strategies.*

## Algorithm 2a: Classical CGT Update

The standard parameter update from Cartis, Gould, and Toint (2011). Simple, robust, and theoretically well-understood.

```
ALGORITHM 2a: Classical CGT Update
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  σ_k         Current regularization parameter
  ρ_k         Ratio of actual to predicted reduction
  η_1, η_2    Thresholds (0 < η_1 < η_2 < 1)
  γ           Multiplicative factor (γ > 1)
  σ_min       Minimum allowed σ
  σ_max       Maximum allowed σ

OUTPUT:
  σ_{k+1}     Updated regularization parameter

PROCEDURE:
  IF ρ_k ≥ η_2 THEN
    σ_{k+1} ← max(σ_k / γ, σ_min)     [very successful]
  ELSE IF ρ_k ≥ η_1 THEN
    σ_{k+1} ← σ_k                      [successful]
  ELSE
    σ_{k+1} ← min(γ · σ_k, σ_max)     [unsuccessful]
  END IF

  RETURN σ_{k+1}
```

## Algorithm 2b: Interpolation-Enhanced CGT Update

Variant that estimates local Lipschitz constant from model-function discrepancy (Gould, Porcelli, Toint, 2012). May provide faster adaptation on large-scale problems with varying curvature.

```
ALGORITHM 2b: Interpolation-Enhanced CGT Update
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  σ_k         Current regularization parameter
  ρ_k         Ratio of actual to predicted reduction
  s_k         Current step
  f(x_k+s_k)  Function value at trial point
  m_k(s_k)    Model value at step
  η_1, η_2    Thresholds
  γ           Multiplicative factor
  σ_min       Minimum allowed σ
  σ_max       Maximum allowed σ

OUTPUT:
  σ_{k+1}     Updated regularization parameter

PROCEDURE:
  1. ESTIMATE LOCAL LIPSCHITZ CONSTANT:
     L̂_k ← 2 |f(x_k + s_k) - m_k(s_k)| / ||s_k||³

  2. UPDATE WITH INTERPOLATION:
     IF ρ_k ≥ η_2 THEN
       σ_{k+1} ← max(L̂_k / 2, σ_k / γ, σ_min)     [very successful]
     ELSE IF ρ_k ≥ η_1 THEN
       σ_{k+1} ← σ_k                               [successful]
     ELSE
       σ_{k+1} ← max(L̂_k / 2, γ · σ_k)            [unsuccessful]
       σ_{k+1} ← min(σ_{k+1}, σ_max)
     END IF

  RETURN σ_{k+1}
```

**Default Parameters**:

| Parameter | Default | Valid Range | Description |
|-----------|---------|-------------|-------------|
| `sigma_init` | 1.0 | $(0, \infty)$ | Initial $\sigma_0$ |
| `eta_1` | 0.1 | $(0, \eta_2)$ | Acceptance threshold |
| `eta_2` | 0.9 | $(\eta_1, 1)$ | Very successful threshold |
| `gamma` | 2.0 | $(1, \infty)$ | Multiplicative factor |
| `sigma_min` | $10^{-12}$ | $(0, \sigma_0)$ | Lower bound |
| `sigma_max` | $10^{12}$ | $(\sigma_0, \infty)$ | Upper bound |

# Momentum Acceleration

Momentum acceleration can reduce iteration counts by 30-90% without additional function evaluations. The adaptive momentum parameter ensures stability by vanishing near convergence.

*Cross-reference: See literature-review.qmd Section 4 for theoretical background on momentum with cubic regularization (CRm, ARCm variants) and complexity analysis.*

## Algorithm 3: ARCm (Momentum-Accelerated ARC)

```
ALGORITHM 3: ARCm (Momentum-Accelerated ARC)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  x_0           Initial point
  σ_0           Initial regularization
  β_max         Maximum momentum parameter
  c_1, c_2      Momentum scaling constants
  [Standard ARC parameters: η_1, η_2, γ, σ_min, σ_max]

OUTPUT:
  x*            Approximate minimizer

PROCEDURE:
  k ← 0
  y_0 ← x_0

  WHILE NOT converged DO    [see Algorithm 0]

    1. EVALUATE at x_k:
       f_k ← f(x_k), g_k ← ∇f(x_k), H_k ← ∇²f(x_k)

    2. SOLVE CUBIC SUBPROBLEM:
       s_k ← argmin_s m_k(s)

    3. SET TRIAL POINT:
       y_{k+1} ← x_k + s_k

    4. COMPUTE ρ_k AND UPDATE σ_k:
       [Standard CGT update - see Algorithm 2a]

    5. APPLY MOMENTUM (if step accepted):
       IF ρ_k ≥ η_1 THEN
         β_k ← min(β_max, c_1/||s_k||, c_2/||g(y_{k+1})||)
         x_{k+1} ← y_{k+1} + β_k · (y_{k+1} - y_k)
       ELSE
         x_{k+1} ← x_k          [no momentum on rejected steps]
         y_{k+1} ← y_k
       END IF

    k ← k + 1
  END WHILE

  RETURN x_k
```

**Momentum Parameters**:

| Parameter | Default | Valid Range | Description |
|-----------|---------|-------------|-------------|
| `momentum_max` | 0.9 | $(0, 1)$ | Maximum $\beta_k$ |
| `momentum_c1` | 0.1 | $(0, \infty)$ | Step-size scaling |
| `momentum_c2` | 0.1 | $(0, \infty)$ | Gradient scaling |

# Quasi-Newton Updates

When analytic Hessians are unavailable and finite differences are too expensive, quasi-Newton approximations provide an alternative. SR1 is preferred over BFGS with ARC because it can represent indefinite curvature.

*Cross-reference: See literature-review.qmd Section 3 for comparison of SR1 vs BFGS, and Section 3.3 for SR1 safeguarding strategies (skip rule, restart, modified variants).*

## Algorithm 4: SR1 Update with Safeguards

```
ALGORITHM 4: SR1 Update with Safeguards
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  B_k         Current Hessian approximation
  s_k         Step taken: s_k = x_{k+1} - x_k
  y_k         Gradient difference: y_k = g_{k+1} - g_k
  τ           Skip threshold (e.g., 10⁻⁸ to 10⁻⁴)
  N_skip      Maximum consecutive skips before restart
  skip_count  Counter of consecutive skipped updates

OUTPUT:
  B_{k+1}     Updated Hessian approximation
  skip_count  Updated skip counter

PROCEDURE:
  1. COMPUTE UPDATE VECTORS:
     r_k ← y_k - B_k s_k
     denom ← r_k^T s_k

  2. CHECK SKIP CONDITION:
     IF |denom| < τ · ||r_k|| · ||s_k|| THEN
       B_{k+1} ← B_k                    [skip update]
       skip_count ← skip_count + 1
     ELSE
       B_{k+1} ← B_k + (r_k r_k^T) / denom    [apply SR1]
       skip_count ← 0
     END IF

  3. CHECK FOR RESTART:
     IF skip_count ≥ N_skip THEN
       α ← y_k^T s_k / (s_k^T s_k)      [Barzilai-Borwein scaling]
       IF α ≤ 0 THEN α ← 1 END IF
       B_{k+1} ← α · I                   [restart with scaled identity]
       skip_count ← 0
     END IF

  RETURN B_{k+1}, skip_count
```

**SR1 Safeguard Parameters**:

| Parameter | Default | Valid Range | Description |
|-----------|---------|-------------|-------------|
| `sr1_skip_tol` | $10^{-6}$ | $(10^{-8}, 10^{-4})$ | Denominator threshold $\tau$ |
| `sr1_restart_after` | 5 | $\{3, 4, 5, ...\}$ | Restarts after N skips |
| `sr1_init_scale` | "bb" | "bb", "identity" | Restart scaling method |

**Why SR1 with ARC**:

- SR1 can represent indefinite Hessians (negative eigenvalues)
- ARC handles indefinite curvature naturally via cubic regularization
- BFGS forces positive definiteness, wasting ARC's saddle-escape capability

# Cubic Subproblem Solver

The cubic subproblem $\min_s m_k(s)$ is the computational core of ARC. We use LDL factorization (`fastmatrix::ldl`) to estimate the minimum eigenvalue shift needed, then iterate on the secular equation to find the optimal regularization multiplier.

*Cross-reference: See literature-review.qmd Section 5.2 for handling indefinite Hessians.*

## Algorithm 5: Modified Cholesky Cubic Solver

The key insight is that LDL decomposition gives diagonal elements that approximate eigenvalue information. When any diagonal element is negative, the matrix is indefinite. The minimum diagonal gives a lower bound on the shift needed.

```
ALGORITHM 5: Modified Cholesky Cubic Solver
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  g           Gradient
  H           Hessian (may be positive definite or indefinite)
  σ           Regularization parameter
  tol_lambda  Convergence tolerance for λ iteration (e.g., 1e-10)
  max_iter    Maximum λ iterations (e.g., 50)
  max_chol_retries  Maximum Cholesky retry attempts (e.g., 5)

OUTPUT:
  s           Solution to cubic subproblem
  λ           Final regularization multiplier
  converged   TRUE if secular equation converged

PROCEDURE:
  0. HANDLE ZERO GRADIENT (early return):
     IF ||g|| < machine_epsilon THEN
       RETURN (s = 0, λ = 0, converged = TRUE)
     END IF

  1. LDL FACTORIZATION FOR SHIFT ESTIMATION:
     (L, D, _) ← ldl(H)
     [H = L D L^T where D is diagonal]
     λ_lower ← max(0, -min(diag(D)))
     [If min(D) < 0, H is indefinite and needs shift ≥ |min(D)|]

  2. COMPUTE INITIAL SHIFT WITH ROBUST BUFFER:
     sqrt_eps ← sqrt(machine_epsilon)    [≈ 1.49e-8]
     ε ← max(sqrt_eps, sqrt_eps × λ_lower)
     λ ← λ_lower + ε
     [Buffer ensures H + λI is strictly PD, not just semi-definite]

  3. INITIAL CHOLESKY WITH RETRY:
     L_λ ← NULL
     FOR attempt = 1 TO max_chol_retries DO
       L_λ ← TRY chol(H + λ I)
       IF L_λ succeeded THEN BREAK END IF
       λ ← λ × 2    [double shift and retry]
     END FOR
     IF L_λ = NULL THEN
       ERROR("Failed to compute Cholesky factorization")
     END IF

  4. INITIAL SOLVE:
     s ← -L_λ^{-T} L_λ^{-1} g    [forward/backward substitution]

  5. ITERATE (secular equation):
     converged ← FALSE
     FOR iter = 1 TO max_iter DO
       [Secular equation: find λ such that ||s(λ)|| = λ/σ]
       φ ← ||s|| - λ/σ

       IF |φ| < tol_lambda × max(1, λ/σ) THEN
         converged ← TRUE
         BREAK
       END IF

       [Newton update on secular equation]
       [φ'(λ) = -s^T (H + λI)^{-1} s / ||s|| - 1/σ]
       w ← L_λ^{-T} s             [solve L_λ^T w = s]
       φ_prime ← -||w||² / ||s|| - 1/σ

       λ_new ← λ - φ / φ_prime
       λ_new ← max(λ_new, λ_lower)    [stay in valid region]

       [Recompute L and s for new λ with retry on failure]
       chol_success ← FALSE
       FOR chol_attempt = 1 TO max_chol_retries DO
         L_new ← TRY chol(H + λ_new I)
         IF L_new succeeded THEN
           L_λ ← L_new
           chol_success ← TRUE
           BREAK
         END IF
         λ_new ← λ_new × 2    [double shift and retry]
       END FOR
       IF NOT chol_success THEN
         BREAK    [use current solution]
       END IF

       s ← -L_λ^{-T} L_λ^{-1} g
       λ ← λ_new
     END FOR

  6. COMPUTE PREDICTED REDUCTION:
     pred_reduction ← -g^T s - ½ s^T H s - (σ/3) ||s||³

  RETURN (s, λ, pred_reduction, converged)

NOTES:
  - LDL is called once at O(n³); each λ iteration is also O(n³)
  - Typically converges in 3-10 iterations
  - Zero gradient returns immediately (optimal step is s = 0)
  - Retry logic handles edge cases where initial shift is insufficient
  - sqrt(machine_epsilon) buffer prevents near-singular regularized Hessians
```

## The Secular Equation

The secular equation $\phi(\lambda) = 0$ arises from the optimality condition. Geometrically, it finds where the regularized Newton step has the right norm:

$$\phi(\lambda) = \|s(\lambda)\| - \frac{\lambda}{\sigma} = 0$$

where $s(\lambda) = -(H + \lambda I)^{-1}g$.

**Properties:**
- $\phi(\lambda) \to +\infty$ as $\lambda \to -\lambda_{\min}^+$ (smallest eigenvalue)
- $\phi(\lambda) \to -\infty$ as $\lambda \to +\infty$
- $\phi$ is strictly decreasing for $\lambda > -\lambda_{\min}$
- Unique root exists in $(\max(0, -\lambda_{\min}), +\infty)$

**LDL advantage:** Instead of computing eigenvalues to find $\lambda_{\min}$, we use LDL decomposition which gives diagonal elements approximating eigenvalue information. The lower bound $\lambda_{\text{lower}} = \max(0, -\min(D))$ ensures we start with a shift large enough to make the regularized Hessian positive definite.

# Robustness Safeguards

These algorithms detect and recover from numerical pathologies that could cause optimizer failure.

*Cross-reference: See literature-review.qmd Section 5 for comprehensive coverage of safeguarding strategies including stagnation detection, NaN/Inf handling, and indefiniteness detection.*

## Algorithm 6a: Stagnation Detection and Recovery

```
ALGORITHM 6a: Stagnation Detection and Recovery
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  step_history      Recent ||s_k|| values (ring buffer)
  f_history         Recent f_k values (ring buffer)
  tol_step          Step size tolerance
  tol_f             Function change tolerance
  max_stagnant      Maximum stagnant iterations before action
  is_qn             TRUE if using quasi-Newton

OUTPUT:
  action            "continue", "refresh_hessian", or "stop"

PROCEDURE:
  1. CHECK STEP SIZE STAGNATION:
     stagnant_steps ← count consecutive ||s_k|| < tol_step

  2. CHECK FUNCTION STAGNATION:
     stagnant_f ← count consecutive |f_{k-1} - f_k| < tol_f

  3. DETERMINE ACTION:
     IF stagnant_steps ≥ max_stagnant OR stagnant_f ≥ max_stagnant THEN
       IF is_qn AND NOT already_refreshed THEN
         RETURN "refresh_hessian"    [try FD Hessian refresh]
       ELSE
         RETURN "stop"               [declare stagnation]
       END IF
     END IF

     RETURN "continue"
```

## Algorithm 6b: NaN/Inf Protection

```
ALGORITHM 6b: NaN/Inf Protection
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  x_trial           Trial point x_k + s_k
  max_nan_retries   Maximum retry attempts
  nan_sigma_factor  σ multiplier on NaN (e.g., 10)

OUTPUT:
  x_{k+1}           Next iterate (or stop signal)
  σ_{k+1}           Updated regularization

PROCEDURE:
  retry_count ← 0

  WHILE retry_count < max_nan_retries DO
    1. EVALUATE at trial point:
       f_trial ← f(x_trial)
       g_trial ← ∇f(x_trial)

    2. CHECK for NaN/Inf:
       IF is_finite(f_trial) AND is_finite(g_trial) THEN
         RETURN (x_trial, σ_k, "success")
       END IF

    3. HANDLE failure:
       σ_k ← σ_k × nan_sigma_factor    [increase regularization]
       s_k ← re-solve_cubic(σ_k)       [smaller step]
       x_trial ← x_k + s_k
       retry_count ← retry_count + 1
  END WHILE

  RETURN (x_k, σ_k, "numerical_failure")   [stop with error]
```

# Constraint Handling

arcopt supports two types of constraints: box constraints (simple bounds) and linear equality constraints. Currently these are mutually exclusive—future versions will support both simultaneously.

## Box Constraints

Box constraints restrict parameters to lie within bounds: $\ell \leq x \leq u$ where $\ell, u \in \mathbb{R}^n$ (use $-\infty$ or $+\infty$ for unbounded dimensions).

The approach uses **projected step with truncated line search**: compute the maximum feasible step length, truncate the step, and project the result for numerical safety.

*Cross-reference: See literature-review.qmd for discussion of bound-constrained optimization and active set methods.*

### Algorithm 7a: Apply Box Constraints to Step

```
ALGORITHM 7a: Apply Box Constraints to Step
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  x_k         Current iterate
  s_k         Proposed step (unconstrained)
  lower       Lower bounds ℓ (use -∞ for unbounded)
  upper       Upper bounds u (use +∞ for unbounded)

OUTPUT:
  s_bounded   Truncated step that keeps x_k + s within bounds
  x_new       New iterate projected to feasible region

PROCEDURE:
  1. COMPUTE MAXIMUM FEASIBLE STEP LENGTH:
     α_max ← 1.0
     FOR i = 1 TO n DO
       IF s_k[i] > 0 AND upper[i] < +∞ THEN
         α_max ← min(α_max, (upper[i] - x_k[i]) / s_k[i])
       ELSE IF s_k[i] < 0 AND lower[i] > -∞ THEN
         α_max ← min(α_max, (lower[i] - x_k[i]) / s_k[i])
       END IF
     END FOR

  2. TRUNCATE STEP:
     s_bounded ← α_max × s_k

  3. PROJECT FOR NUMERICAL SAFETY:
     x_new ← project_to_box(x_k + s_bounded, lower, upper)

  RETURN s_bounded, x_new


FUNCTION project_to_box(x, lower, upper):
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  FOR i = 1 TO n DO
    x[i] ← max(lower[i], min(upper[i], x[i]))
  END FOR
  RETURN x
```

### Algorithm 7b: Validate and Project Initial Point

```
ALGORITHM 7b: Validate and Project Initial Point
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  x_0         User-provided initial point
  lower       Lower bounds
  upper       Upper bounds
  warn        Whether to warn if projection needed

OUTPUT:
  x_0_proj    Feasible initial point

PROCEDURE:
  1. VALIDATE BOUNDS:
     IF any(lower > upper) THEN
       ERROR("Invalid bounds: lower > upper")
     END IF
     IF length(lower) ≠ n OR length(upper) ≠ n THEN
       ERROR("Bounds dimension mismatch")
     END IF

  2. CHECK FEASIBILITY:
     is_feasible ← all(lower ≤ x_0 ≤ upper)

  3. PROJECT IF NEEDED:
     IF NOT is_feasible THEN
       IF warn THEN
         WARN("Initial point infeasible, projecting to bounds")
       END IF
       x_0_proj ← project_to_box(x_0, lower, upper)
     ELSE
       x_0_proj ← x_0
     END IF

  RETURN x_0_proj
```

### Integration with Algorithm 1

In the main ARC loop (Algorithm 1), box constraints are applied at two points:

1. **After computing step** (Steps 3-4): Replace `s_k` with truncated step
2. **After update** (Step 7): Ensure `x_{k+1}` is projected to feasible region

```
[In Algorithm 1, after step computation:]

IF bounds provided THEN
  (s_k, x_trial) ← apply_box_constraints(x_k, s_k, lower, upper)
END IF
```

## Linear Equality Constraints

Linear equality constraints have the form $Ax = b$ where $A$ is $m \times n$ with $m < n$ and $\text{rank}(A) = m$.

The approach uses the **null space method**: reparameterize the problem to optimize over a lower-dimensional unconstrained space.

*Cross-reference: See literature-review.qmd for discussion of equality-constrained optimization and null space methods.*

### Algorithm 8a: Compute Null Space Basis

```
ALGORITHM 8a: Compute Null Space Basis
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  A           Constraint matrix (m × n)
  b           Right-hand side vector (m × 1)

OUTPUT:
  x_p         Particular solution satisfying Ax_p = b
  Z           Null space basis (n × (n-m)) where AZ = 0

PROCEDURE:
  1. VALIDATE CONSTRAINTS:
     (m, n) ← dim(A)
     IF m ≥ n THEN
       ERROR("Too many constraints: m must be < n")
     END IF
     IF rank(A) < m THEN
       ERROR("Rank-deficient constraint matrix")
     END IF

  2. COMPUTE QR DECOMPOSITION of A^T:
     [Q, R] ← qr(A^T)
     [Q is n × n orthogonal, R is n × m upper triangular]

  3. EXTRACT NULL SPACE BASIS:
     Z ← Q[:, (m+1):n]    [last (n-m) columns of Q]
     [Z is n × (n-m), satisfies AZ = 0]

  4. COMPUTE PARTICULAR SOLUTION:
     x_p ← A^T (A A^T)^{-1} b    [minimum-norm solution]
     [Or via QR: x_p ← Q[:, 1:m] × solve(R[1:m, :], b)]

  RETURN x_p, Z

NOTES:
  - Any feasible x can be written as x = x_p + Z z for some z ∈ ℝ^{n-m}
  - Optimization over z is unconstrained and lower-dimensional
  - Gradient transforms: ∇_z f = Z^T ∇_x f
  - Hessian transforms: ∇²_z f = Z^T ∇²_x f Z
```

### Algorithm 8b: Transform Between Spaces

```
ALGORITHM 8b: Transform Between x-space and z-space
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

FUNCTION transform_to_z_space(x, x_p, Z):
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  [Project x onto null space coordinates]
  z ← Z^T (x - x_p)
  RETURN z

FUNCTION transform_to_x_space(z, x_p, Z):
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  [Reconstruct x from null space coordinates]
  x ← x_p + Z z
  RETURN x

FUNCTION wrap_objective_for_z_space(fn, x_p, Z):
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  [Create wrapper that transforms objective and derivatives]

  FUNCTION fn_z(z):
    x ← x_p + Z z
    (f, g_x) ← fn(x)
    g_z ← Z^T g_x    [chain rule]
    RETURN (f, g_z)

  RETURN fn_z

FUNCTION wrap_hessian_for_z_space(hess_fn, x_p, Z):
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  [Create wrapper that transforms Hessian]

  FUNCTION hess_z(z):
    x ← x_p + Z z
    H_x ← hess_fn(x)
    H_z ← Z^T H_x Z    [congruence transformation]
    RETURN H_z

  RETURN hess_z
```

### Algorithm 8c: Project to Constraint Manifold

```
ALGORITHM 8c: Project to Constraint Manifold
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

INPUT:
  x           Point (possibly infeasible)
  A           Constraint matrix
  b           Right-hand side

OUTPUT:
  x_proj      Closest point satisfying Ax = b

PROCEDURE:
  1. COMPUTE CONSTRAINT VIOLATION:
     r ← A x - b

  2. IF ||r|| < tol THEN
     RETURN x    [already feasible]
  END IF

  3. PROJECT (minimum-norm correction):
     x_proj ← x - A^T (A A^T)^{-1} r

  RETURN x_proj
```

### Integration with Algorithm 1

When linear equality constraints are provided, Algorithm 1 operates in reduced z-space:

```
[Before main loop:]
IF eq_constraints provided THEN
  (x_p, Z) ← compute_null_space_basis(A, b)
  x_0 ← project_to_constraint_manifold(x_0, A, b)
  z_0 ← transform_to_z_space(x_0, x_p, Z)
  fn_z ← wrap_objective_for_z_space(fn, x_p, Z)
  hess_z ← wrap_hessian_for_z_space(hess, x_p, Z)
  [Run optimization on fn_z starting from z_0]
END IF

[After convergence:]
x* ← transform_to_x_space(z*, x_p, Z)
```

**Key properties:**
- Dimension reduced from n to (n - rank(A))
- All iterates automatically satisfy Ax = b
- Gradients and Hessians transformed via chain rule
- Return value includes `reduced_dimension` and `constraint_satisfied` fields

### Constraint Parameters

| Parameter | Default | Description |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| `bounds$lower` | NULL | Lower bounds vector (use -Inf for unbounded) |
| `bounds$upper` | NULL | Upper bounds vector (use +Inf for unbounded) |
| `eq_constraints$A` | NULL | Constraint matrix (m × n) |
| `eq_constraints$b` | NULL | Right-hand side vector |
| `constraint_tol` | $10^{-10}$ | Tolerance for constraint satisfaction |

### Current Limitations

- **Phase 1**: Either bounds OR eq_constraints, not both simultaneously
- Combined box + linear equality constraints planned for future release
- Nonlinear constraints not yet supported

# Complete Parameter Reference

## All Control Parameters

| Parameter | Default | Algorithm | Description |
|-----------|---------|-----------|-------------|
| **Objective Scaling** ||||
| `fnscale` | 1 | Core ARC | Scaling factor (1 = minimize, -1 = maximize) |
| **Diagnostics** ||||
| `refresh` | 10 | Output | Print frequency (0 = silent) |
| `trace` | 1 | Output | Diagnostic storage level (0-3) |
| **Convergence (Stan-style)** ||||
| `tol_grad` | $10^{-8}$ | Convergence | Absolute gradient tolerance |
| `tol_rel_grad` | $10^{7}$ | Convergence | Relative gradient tolerance |
| `tol_obj` | $10^{-12}$ | Convergence | Absolute objective change |
| `tol_rel_obj` | $10^{4}$ | Convergence | Relative objective change |
| `tol_param` | $10^{-8}$ | Convergence | Parameter change tolerance |
| `max_iter` | 1000 | Convergence | Maximum iterations |
| **Regularization** ||||
| `sigma_init` | 1.0 | Core ARC | Initial $\sigma_0$ |
| `eta_1` | 0.1 | Core ARC | Acceptance threshold |
| `eta_2` | 0.9 | Core ARC | Very successful threshold |
| `gamma` | 2.0 | Core ARC | Update factor |
| `sigma_min` | $10^{-12}$ | Core ARC | Lower bound |
| `sigma_max` | $10^{12}$ | Core ARC | Upper bound |
| `use_interpolation` | FALSE | CGT | Enable interpolation variant |
| **Cubic Solver** ||||
| `tol_lambda` | $10^{-10}$ | Algorithm 5 | λ iteration convergence tolerance |
| `max_iter_lambda` | 100 | Algorithm 5 | Maximum λ iterations |
| **Newton Step** ||||
| `try_newton` | TRUE | Core ARC | Attempt Newton before cubic |
| `c_armijo` | $10^{-4}$ | Newton | Armijo sufficient decrease |
| **Momentum** ||||
| `use_momentum` | FALSE | ARCm | Enable momentum |
| `momentum_max` | 0.9 | ARCm | Maximum $\beta$ |
| `momentum_c1` | 0.1 | ARCm | Step scaling constant |
| `momentum_c2` | 0.1 | ARCm | Gradient scaling constant |
| **Quasi-Newton** ||||
| `hess_method` | "fd" | SR1/BFGS | "analytic", "fd", "sr1", "bfgs" |
| `sr1_skip_tol` | $10^{-6}$ | SR1 | Denominator threshold |
| `sr1_restart_after` | 5 | SR1 | Skips before restart |
| `sr1_init_scale` | "bb" | SR1 | Restart scaling |
| **Step Bounds** ||||
| `step_max` | NULL | Safeguard | Maximum step (NULL = adaptive) |
| `step_max_factor` | 10 | Safeguard | Adaptive factor |
| **Robustness** ||||
| `max_stagnant` | 5 | Stagnation | Iterations before action |
| `stagnation_reset` | TRUE | Stagnation | Reset QN on stagnation |
| `max_nan_retries` | 3 | NaN/Inf | Retry attempts |
| `nan_sigma_factor` | 10 | NaN/Inf | σ multiplier on NaN |
| **Constraints** ||||
| `bounds$lower` | NULL | Box | Lower bounds (use -Inf for unbounded) |
| `bounds$upper` | NULL | Box | Upper bounds (use +Inf for unbounded) |
| `eq_constraints$A` | NULL | Linear Eq | Constraint matrix (m × n) |
| `eq_constraints$b` | NULL | Linear Eq | Right-hand side vector |
| `constraint_tol` | $10^{-10}$ | Constraints | Tolerance for satisfaction check |

# Implementation Changelog

This section documents significant changes made to the pseudocode based on implementation experience and benchmark testing.

| Date | Algorithm | Change | Problem | Rationale |
|------|-----------|--------|---------|-----------|
| 2025-01 | Algorithm 5 | Added zero gradient early return (Step 0) | When gradient is exactly zero, secular equation iteration fails to converge because $\|s\| = 0$ cannot satisfy $\|s\| = \lambda/\sigma$ for any $\lambda > 0$ | Zero gradient implies optimality; returning $s = 0$, $\lambda = 0$ immediately is mathematically correct and avoids unnecessary computation |
| 2025-01 | Algorithm 5 | Changed buffer from $\varepsilon = 10^{-12}$ to $\sqrt{\text{machine\_epsilon}}$ (Step 2) | With buffer $10^{-12}$, when $\lambda_{\text{lower}} = -\lambda_{\min}(H)$ exactly, the regularized Hessian $H + \lambda I$ has a near-zero eigenvalue, causing Cholesky to fail with "leading minor not positive" | $\sqrt{\text{machine\_epsilon}} \approx 1.49 \times 10^{-8}$ is the standard numerical tolerance for detecting near-singularity; ensures $H + \lambda I$ is strictly positive definite, not just semi-definite |
| 2025-01 | Algorithm 5 | Added Cholesky retry logic with exponential backoff (Steps 3, 5) | Even with sqrt(eps) buffer, edge cases with ill-conditioned Hessians can still cause Cholesky failure | Retry with doubled $\lambda$ (up to 5 attempts) provides graceful degradation; larger regularization always makes the problem more well-conditioned |
| 2025-01 | Algorithm 5 | Changed from `mchol` to `ldl` for shift estimation (Step 1) | Implementation uses `fastmatrix::ldl()` which directly provides diagonal D for eigenvalue estimation | LDL decomposition gives $\lambda_{\text{lower}} = \max(0, -\min(D))$ directly; simpler than extracting perturbation from modified Cholesky |

## Benchmark Validation

These changes were validated on 80 test cases (8 benchmark functions × 10 starting points):

| Metric | Before Fix | After Fix |
|--------|------------|-----------|
| Converged | 77/80 (96.3%) | 80/80 (100%) |
| Correct (err < 1e-4) | 53/80 (66.3%) | 56/80 (70.0%) |
| Errors/Crashes | 3 | 0 |

Comparison with `trust::trust` package showed arcopt is competitive:
- arcopt: 56/80 correct, avg 7.6 iterations (when both succeed)
- trust: 55/80 correct, avg 8.6 iterations (when both succeed)

# References

- Cartis, C., Gould, N. I. M., & Toint, P. L. (2011). Adaptive cubic regularisation methods for unconstrained optimization. Part I: motivation, convergence and numerical results. *Mathematical Programming*, 127(2), 245-295.

- Gould, N. I. M., Porcelli, M., & Toint, P. L. (2012). Updating the regularization parameter in the adaptive cubic regularization algorithm. *Computational Optimization and Applications*, 53(1), 1-22.

- Wang, Z., Zhou, Y., Liang, Y., & Lan, G. (2018). Cubic regularization with momentum for nonconvex optimization. *UAI 2018*.

- Benson, H. Y., & Shanno, D. F. (2018). Cubic regularization in symmetric rank-1 quasi-Newton methods. *Mathematical Programming Computation*, 10(4), 457-486.

- Nocedal, J., & Wright, S. J. (2006). *Numerical Optimization* (2nd ed.). Springer.

- Stan Development Team. CmdStan User's Guide: Optimization. https://mc-stan.org/docs/cmdstan-guide/optimize_config.html
